Thanks for the call today, Patrícia. Overall, your demo was a real strength.
Thank you, David. I really appreciate that. Is there any area for improvement?
Perhaps it might be better to slow down a little on the pricing slide.
That is a fair point. I acknowledge that. I did rush it a bit.
And have you considered sharing the deck before the call?
Good idea. My takeaway is to send it earlier and align on the agenda. May I share my perspective on the timing too?

Hi Patrícia, this is David, leaving you a quick note before our one-on-one tomorrow. I just want to say that, overall, your work this quarter has been a real strength for the team. When we talk, I will share one small area for improvement, but nothing serious, so please do not worry. I always try to give feedback in a constructive way. Perhaps we could look at how you run the pricing part of the call, because it might be better to slow down a little there. I also really want to hear your perspective, because good feedback should go both ways. My main takeaway from this quarter is simple: you have grown a lot, and I appreciate everything you bring to the team. See you tomorrow.

Hi Patrícia, it is Sofia. I wanted to share something with you after my own review last week. Honestly, receiving feedback used to make me nervous, but I have learned to see it differently. Now, when a manager points out an area for improvement, I try to acknowledge it calmly and simply say thank you. I ask one or two questions, so I really understand their perspective, and then we agree on one clear takeaway. The trick is not to take it personally. Good feedback is constructive; it is there to help you grow, not to criticize you. So when we give feedback to our own customers, let us remember to lead with a strength first, and to keep the tone warm and respectful. Talk soon.
